title: Feelings & Reactions
description: Beyond happy and sad — the specific emotions that real conversations require.

words.example: Ich habe Sehnsucht nach meiner Familie.
words.exampleTranslation: I miss my family.
words.whyItMatters: Iconic German word — deeper than 'miss'. 'Sehnsucht haben nach + Dativ' = to long for. Often used in literature and music.

words.example: Die Kinder langweilen sich im Auto.
words.exampleTranslation: The children are bored in the car.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive verb — 'I am boring myself'. Don't say 'Ich bin langweilig' (that means 'I am a boring person'!). Perfekt: 'haben sich gelangweilt'.

words.example: Er wird leicht eifersüchtig.
words.exampleTranslation: He gets jealous easily.
words.whyItMatters: Specifically romantic/possessive jealousy. For envy of things use 'neidisch'. Takes 'auf + Akkusativ': 'eifersüchtig auf den Kollegen'.

words.example: Er bleibt immer gelassen, auch unter Stress.
words.exampleTranslation: He always stays calm, even under stress.
words.whyItMatters: Past participle of 'lassen' used as adjective — implies 'having let things go'. Stronger than 'ruhig' (quiet/calm).

words.example: Ich war erstaunt über das Ergebnis.
words.exampleTranslation: I was astonished at the result.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'über + Akkusativ' for the source. Stronger than 'überrascht' (just surprised). The noun is 'das Erstaunen'.

words.example: Wir waren von dem Film enttäuscht.
words.exampleTranslation: We were disappointed by the film.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'von + Dativ' for the source. Literally 'un-deceived' — implies your expectations were broken.

words.example: Ich bin stolz auf meine Schwester.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm proud of my sister.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'auf + Akkusativ'. Noun 'der Stolz' (pride). Cultural note: open displays of pride are less common in German culture than in US/Indian culture.

words.example: Sei nicht neidisch auf seinen Erfolg!
words.exampleTranslation: Don't be envious of his success!
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'auf + Akkusativ'. For things/situations (not romantic). The noun 'der Neid' is one of the seven deadly sins.

words.example: Sie ist verliebt in einen Kollegen.
words.exampleTranslation: She's in love with a colleague.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'in + Akkusativ'. From 'sich verlieben' (to fall in love). Difference: 'verliebt sein' (be in love) vs 'lieben' (to love deeply).

words.example: Ich bin so aufgeregt vor der Prüfung!
words.exampleTranslation: I'm so nervous before the exam!
words.whyItMatters: Ambiguous: can mean excited (positive) OR nervous (negative). Context decides. From 'sich aufregen' (to get worked up).

words.example: Das war wirklich peinlich für mich.
words.exampleTranslation: That was really embarrassing for me.
words.whyItMatters: Describes the situation, not the person feeling it. 'Es ist mir peinlich' (dative) = I'm embarrassed.

words.example: Schade, dass du nicht kommen kannst.
words.exampleTranslation: Too bad you can't come.
words.whyItMatters: Mild disappointment expression — used constantly in spoken German. Often stands alone as a reaction: 'Schade!'

title: Travel & Transport
description: Real-world vocabulary for booking, navigating, and surviving German trains, hotels, and trips.

words.example: In Frankfurt müssen Sie umsteigen.
words.exampleTranslation: You have to change trains in Frankfurt.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich steige in Frankfurt um'. Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'bin umgestiegen'. Essential word for German train travel.

words.example: Wir haben unseren Anschluss verpasst.
words.exampleTranslation: We missed our connection.
words.whyItMatters: Specifically a connecting train/bus/flight. Different from 'die Verbindung' (general connection). 'Anschlusszug' = connecting train.

words.example: Gibt es eine direkte Verbindung nach Berlin?
words.exampleTranslation: Is there a direct connection to Berlin?
words.whyItMatters: Used for overall routes — phone/internet connections too. 'Eine gute Verbindung haben' = to have a good connection.

words.example: Die Stadt hat viele Sehenswürdigkeiten.
words.exampleTranslation: The city has many tourist attractions.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'sehen' (to see) + 'wert' (worth) + '-igkeit'. Almost always used in plural. The classic German monster-word — practice saying it!

words.example: Wir suchen noch eine Unterkunft für die Nacht.
words.exampleTranslation: We're still looking for accommodation for the night.
words.whyItMatters: More formal than 'das Hotel' — covers hotels, hostels, Airbnb, anything. Plural 'die Unterkünfte' (with umlaut).

words.example: Ich habe das Hotel online gebucht.
words.exampleTranslation: I booked the hotel online.
words.whyItMatters: Regular verb — Perfekt 'hat gebucht'. Used for flights, hotels, restaurants, anything. Noun 'die Buchung' (booking).

words.example: Der Zug hat zwanzig Minuten Verspätung.
words.exampleTranslation: The train is twenty minutes late.
words.whyItMatters: Use 'Verspätung haben' — Germans say 'the train has delay'. Verb 'sich verspäten' (to be late) for people.

words.example: Die Strecke nach München ist sehr schön.
words.exampleTranslation: The route to Munich is very beautiful.
words.whyItMatters: Used for routes/distances in travel. 'Eine lange Strecke fahren' (to drive a long way). Also: cancelled section = 'gesperrte Strecke'.

words.example: Hast du eine Reiseversicherung abgeschlossen?
words.exampleTranslation: Did you take out travel insurance?
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'die Reise' + 'die Versicherung'. 'Eine Versicherung abschließen' (to take out insurance) is the set phrase.

words.example: Wir wünschen Ihnen einen angenehmen Aufenthalt!
words.exampleTranslation: We wish you a pleasant stay!
words.whyItMatters: Standard hotel greeting. Also 'der Auslandsaufenthalt' (stay abroad) — useful for studying abroad contexts.

words.example: Die Grenze zu Polen ist nur 100 km entfernt.
words.exampleTranslation: The Polish border is only 100 km away.
words.whyItMatters: Also figurative: 'meine Grenzen' (my limits). 'Die Grenze überschreiten' (cross the border / cross a line) works both literally and metaphorically.

words.example: Mein Reisepass ist bald abgelaufen.
words.exampleTranslation: My passport expires soon.
words.whyItMatters: Short form 'der Pass' is common. Important verbs: 'gültig sein' (to be valid), 'ablaufen' (to expire), 'verlängern' (to renew).

title: Work & Office
description: Apply for jobs, talk about your career, and navigate the German workplace.

words.example: Bitte schicken Sie mir Ihren Lebenslauf.
words.exampleTranslation: Please send me your CV.
words.whyItMatters: Literally 'life-course'. The standard format in Germany is tabular and includes a photo (different from US/UK). Plural 'die Lebensläufe'.

words.example: Ich habe meine Bewerbung gestern abgeschickt.
words.exampleTranslation: I sent off my application yesterday.
words.whyItMatters: Includes the cover letter ('das Anschreiben'), CV, and certificates. 'Eine Bewerbung schreiben/schicken/einreichen' are all standard.

words.example: Ich bewerbe mich um eine Stelle bei BMW.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm applying for a position at BMW.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive! Takes 'um + Akkusativ' (for): 'sich bewerben um eine Stelle'. Or 'bei + Dativ' (at): 'bei einer Firma'.

words.example: Sie hat eine Beförderung bekommen.
words.exampleTranslation: She got a promotion.
words.whyItMatters: From 'befördern' (to promote, also: to transport). Different from 'die Erhöhung' (raise/increase). 'Eine Gehaltserhöhung' = a salary raise.

words.example: Wie hoch ist Ihr Gehalt?
words.exampleTranslation: How much is your salary?
words.whyItMatters: Neuter — unusual for a Latin-origin word. Plural 'die Gehälter' (with umlaut). 'Das Gehalt' is monthly fixed pay; 'der Lohn' is wages (often hourly).

words.example: Sie hat ihre Kündigung eingereicht.
words.exampleTranslation: She submitted her resignation.
words.whyItMatters: Works in both directions — you can quit OR be fired with a 'Kündigung'. Verb 'kündigen' = to quit / give notice. Germany has strong worker protection laws around this.

words.example: Die Besprechung beginnt um zehn Uhr.
words.exampleTranslation: The meeting starts at ten.
words.whyItMatters: More formal than 'das Meeting' (English loanword, also widely used). Verb 'besprechen' (to discuss something).

words.example: Ich habe einen Termin beim Zahnarzt.
words.exampleTranslation: I have an appointment at the dentist's.
words.whyItMatters: Cultural staple: Germans book appointments for everything (doctor, hairdresser, government offices). Useful verbs: 'einen Termin machen/vereinbaren/absagen/verschieben'.

words.example: Der Kunde hat sich beschwert.
words.exampleTranslation: The customer complained.
words.whyItMatters: Weak masculine — accusative 'den Kunden', dative 'dem Kunden'. Female 'die Kundin'. 'Der Kunde ist König' = the customer is king.

words.example: Ich arbeite in der Marketing-Abteilung.
words.exampleTranslation: I work in the marketing department.
words.whyItMatters: From 'abteilen' (to divide off). Common compounds: 'Personalabteilung' (HR), 'Buchhaltung' (accounting), 'IT-Abteilung'.

words.example: Endlich Feierabend!
words.exampleTranslation: Finally, end of work!
words.whyItMatters: Iconic German concept — literally 'celebration evening'. 'Feierabend machen' = to call it a day. Said with great satisfaction at 5pm.

words.example: Ich habe diese Woche viele Überstunden gemacht.
words.exampleTranslation: I worked a lot of overtime this week.
words.whyItMatters: Usually plural: 'Überstunden machen' (to work overtime). German labor law strictly regulates and often compensates these.

title: Home & Neighborhood
description: Rent, neighbors, renovation — the vocabulary of actually living somewhere in Germany.

words.example: Die Miete ist letztes Jahr gestiegen.
words.exampleTranslation: The rent went up last year.
words.whyItMatters: Verb 'mieten' (to rent something); 'vermieten' (to rent out). 'Warmmiete' = rent + utilities; 'Kaltmiete' = rent only. Crucial distinction!

words.example: Unser Vermieter ist sehr nett.
words.exampleTranslation: Our landlord is very nice.
words.whyItMatters: Pair: 'der Vermieter' (landlord) and 'der Mieter' (tenant). Female forms 'die Vermieterin' / 'die Mieterin'. Vermieter = 'one who rents out'.

words.example: Die Renovierung dauert drei Monate.
words.exampleTranslation: The renovation takes three months.
words.whyItMatters: Verb 'renovieren' (Latin-origin, no 'ge-' in Partizip II: 'renoviert'). 'Eine Renovierung machen/durchführen' are standard collocations.

words.example: Unser Balkon ist nach Süden.
words.exampleTranslation: Our balcony faces south.
words.whyItMatters: Pronunciation: 'BAL-kong' (nasal -n, like French). Plural 'die Balkone' or 'die Balkons' — both correct. A balcony adds significantly to rent in Germany.

words.example: Der Aufzug ist heute kaputt.
words.exampleTranslation: The elevator is broken today.
words.whyItMatters: Synonym 'der Fahrstuhl' is just as common. Most German apartment buildings (especially older ones) don't have one — important when flat-hunting!

words.example: Bringst du den Müll runter?
words.exampleTranslation: Will you take the trash down?
words.whyItMatters: Cultural staple: Germans take recycling very seriously. 'Mülltrennung' (sorting trash) is mandatory: Papier, Glas, Plastik, Bio, Restmüll.

words.example: Wir leben in einer ruhigen Nachbarschaft.
words.exampleTranslation: We live in a quiet neighborhood.
words.whyItMatters: Different from 'das Viertel' (district/quarter) — Nachbarschaft is the immediate social neighborhood (your nearby neighbors). Word for 'das Viertel' more about geography.

words.example: Wir ziehen nächsten Monat um.
words.exampleTranslation: We're moving next month.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'wir ziehen um'. Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'sind umgezogen'. Don't confuse with 'sich umziehen' (to change clothes) — reflexive!

words.example: Im Winter ist die Heizung sehr wichtig.
words.exampleTranslation: In winter, heating is very important.
words.whyItMatters: Verb 'heizen' (to heat). 'Die Heizkosten' (heating costs) are a big expense — often paid separately or as part of 'Nebenkosten' (additional fees).

words.example: Die Nebenkosten sind in der Miete enthalten.
words.exampleTranslation: Utilities are included in the rent.
words.whyItMatters: Plural-only. Covers water, heating, trash, building maintenance — but usually NOT electricity ('Strom') or internet. Critical concept for renting in Germany.

words.example: Der Hausmeister hat die Tür repariert.
words.exampleTranslation: The caretaker fixed the door.
words.whyItMatters: Every German apartment building has one. Goes by 'Hausi' colloquially. Responsible for repairs, cleaning shared areas, dealing with complaints.

words.example: Ich wohne im dritten Stock.
words.exampleTranslation: I live on the third floor.
words.whyItMatters: Watch: in Germany 'erster Stock' = first floor ABOVE ground floor (= second floor American). Ground floor is 'das Erdgeschoss' or 'EG'.

title: Connectors & Time Words
description: Beyond weil and aber — the connectors that turn isolated A1 sentences into flowing A2 discourse.

words.example: Ich wollte joggen. Stattdessen habe ich ferngesehen.
words.exampleTranslation: I wanted to jog. Instead I watched TV.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger — verb in position 2: 'Stattdessen habe ich...'. Use to introduce a substitution after stating what didn't happen.

words.example: Ruf mich an, falls du Hilfe brauchst.
words.exampleTranslation: Call me in case you need help.
words.whyItMatters: Subordinating — verb to the end: '...falls du Hilfe brauchst'. More uncertain than 'wenn' — implies 'in the case that' (might not happen).

words.example: Sobald ich zu Hause bin, rufe ich dich an.
words.exampleTranslation: As soon as I'm home, I'll call you.
words.whyItMatters: Subordinating — verb to the end. When the 'sobald'-clause comes first, the main clause begins with the verb (V2 inversion).

words.example: Während ich koche, hört er Musik.
words.exampleTranslation: While I cook, he listens to music.
words.whyItMatters: Subordinating — verb to the end. Also works as a preposition with genitive: 'während des Tages' (during the day).

words.example: Einerseits ist es teuer, andererseits gut.
words.exampleTranslation: On the one hand it's expensive, on the other hand it's good.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first: 'Einerseits ist es...'. Pairs with 'andererseits'. Essential for B1 written exams — start using at A2 to get ahead.

words.example: Es ist teuer, andererseits ist die Qualität gut.
words.exampleTranslation: It's expensive — on the other hand, the quality is good.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first. Partner to 'einerseits'. Together they signal balanced, mature reasoning — exam-ready phrasing.

words.example: Schließlich haben wir uns für das rote Auto entschieden.
words.exampleTranslation: Finally we decided on the red car.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first: 'Schließlich haben wir...'. Two uses: (1) at last/finally, (2) 'after all' (giving a reason: 'Er muss es wissen, schließlich ist er der Chef').

words.example: Übrigens, ich habe deinen Bruder gesehen.
words.exampleTranslation: By the way, I saw your brother.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first. Used to introduce a tangential remark. Equivalent to English 'by the way' or 'incidentally'.

words.example: Es regnete. Trotzdem sind wir spazieren gegangen.
words.exampleTranslation: It was raining. We went for a walk anyway.
words.whyItMatters: Adverb, NOT a conjunction! V2 trigger: 'Trotzdem sind wir...'. Don't confuse with subordinating 'obwohl' (which sends verb to the end).

words.example: Es war kalt. Deswegen sind wir nicht gegangen.
words.exampleTranslation: It was cold. That's why we didn't go.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first: 'Deswegen sind wir...'. Interchangeable with 'deshalb' and 'darum'. All three trigger the same V2 pattern.

words.example: Das Hotel war schlecht. Außerdem war es teuer.
words.exampleTranslation: The hotel was bad. Moreover, it was expensive.
words.whyItMatters: V2 trigger when first. Used to ADD a point in the same direction (good after good, bad after bad). Strong essay connector.

words.example: Es ist zwar teuer, aber sehr gut.
words.exampleTranslation: It is admittedly expensive, but very good.
words.whyItMatters: Two-part connector. 'Zwar' doesn't trigger V2 by itself — 'aber' follows naturally as a coordinating conjunction (no word-order change).

title: Nuanced Adjectives
description: Twelve adjectives that sharpen your descriptions from generic to precise.

words.example: Mein Vater wird schnell ungeduldig.
words.exampleTranslation: My father gets impatient quickly.
words.whyItMatters: Opposite of 'geduldig'. The 'un-' prefix systematically forms opposites in German: glücklich/unglücklich, höflich/unhöflich, etc.

words.example: Ich war überrascht von der Nachricht.
words.exampleTranslation: I was surprised by the news.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'von + Dativ' for the source. Past participle of 'überraschen' (to surprise — takes accusative). Noun 'die Überraschung'.

words.example: Sie war enttäuscht von ihrem Ergebnis.
words.exampleTranslation: She was disappointed by her result.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'von + Dativ'. Literally 'un-deceived' — your expectations were broken. Pair with 'das Ergebnis' (result) or 'der Film' for typical contexts.

words.example: Wir waren beeindruckt von der Aufführung.
words.exampleTranslation: We were impressed by the performance.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'von + Dativ'. Don't confuse with 'eindrucksvoll' (impressive — describes the thing causing the impression).

words.example: Er ist sehr unzuverlässig — kommt immer zu spät.
words.exampleTranslation: He's very unreliable — always comes late.
words.whyItMatters: Opposite 'zuverlässig' (reliable). Often pairs with 'der Kollege', 'die Verbindung', 'die Information'. Negative trait Germans particularly dislike.

words.example: Meine Arbeit ist sehr abwechslungsreich.
words.exampleTranslation: My work is very varied.
words.whyItMatters: Common in job ads. Compound: 'die Abwechslung' (variety) + 'reich' (rich). Opposite: 'eintönig' (monotonous).

words.example: Das ist eine anspruchsvolle Aufgabe.
words.exampleTranslation: That's a demanding task.
words.whyItMatters: Two uses: (1) demanding (a task), (2) discerning (a person with high standards). Common pairings: 'anspruchsvoller Job', 'anspruchsvolles Buch'.

words.example: Sei vernünftig und schlaf etwas.
words.exampleTranslation: Be sensible and get some sleep.
words.whyItMatters: From 'die Vernunft' (reason — a key concept in German philosophy). Praising someone as 'vernünftig' is a high compliment in German culture.

words.example: Seine Reaktion war völlig übertrieben.
words.exampleTranslation: His reaction was totally over-the-top.
words.whyItMatters: Past participle of 'übertreiben' (to exaggerate). Also adverbial: 'übertrieben teuer' (excessively expensive). Common pairings: 'Reaktion', 'Preis', 'Forderung'.

words.example: Sie ist hartnäckig — sie gibt nie auf.
words.exampleTranslation: She's persistent — she never gives up.
words.whyItMatters: Literally 'hard-necked'. Can be positive (persistent) or negative (stubborn). Pairs: 'hartnäckige Erkältung' (persistent cold), 'hartnäckiger Fleck' (stubborn stain).

words.example: Mein Bruder ist immer verlässlich.
words.exampleTranslation: My brother is always reliable.
words.whyItMatters: Near-synonym of 'zuverlässig' — often interchangeable. Pairs with people, partners, sources of information. From 'sich verlassen auf' (to rely on).

words.example: Das Konzert war eindrucksvoll.
words.exampleTranslation: The concert was impressive.
words.whyItMatters: Describes the thing/event making the impression. Person is 'beeindruckt'. Compound: 'der Eindruck' (impression) + 'voll' (full of). Common pairings: 'Leistung', 'Ergebnis', 'Auftritt'.

title: Trennbare Verben
description: Twelve mid-level separable verbs — the workhorses of intermediate German.

words.example: Ich bereite das Essen vor.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm preparing the food.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'er bereitet das Essen vor'. Perfekt: 'hat vorbereitet'. Often reflexive too: 'sich auf eine Prüfung vorbereiten' (prepare for an exam).

words.meaning: to sleep in, to sleep enough
words.example: Am Sonntag schlafe ich aus.
words.exampleTranslation: On Sunday I sleep in.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich schlafe aus'. Vowel change: 'du schläfst aus, er schläft aus'. Perfekt: 'hat ausgeschlafen'.

words.example: Ich ziehe mich schnell an.
words.exampleTranslation: I'll get dressed quickly.
words.whyItMatters: Both reflexive AND separable! Splits: 'ich ziehe mich an'. Without reflexive 'anziehen' = to put on (a specific item). Perfekt: 'hat sich angezogen'.

words.meaning: to lose weight; to pick up (phone)
words.example: Ich möchte fünf Kilo abnehmen.
words.exampleTranslation: I'd like to lose five kilos.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich nehme ab'. Multiple meanings: lose weight, decrease, pick up phone, take away. Perfekt: 'hat abgenommen'.

words.example: Im Winter nehme ich oft zu.
words.exampleTranslation: In winter I often gain weight.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'er nimmt zu'. Opposite of 'abnehmen'. Also: 'die Spannung nimmt zu' (tension increases). Perfekt: 'hat zugenommen'.

words.example: Ich leite dir die E-Mail weiter.
words.exampleTranslation: I'll forward you the email.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich leite die E-Mail weiter'. Daily-use office vocab. Perfekt: 'hat weitergeleitet'. Takes accusative (the email) + dative (the recipient).

words.example: Was soll ich zur Party mitbringen?
words.exampleTranslation: What should I bring to the party?
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich bringe Wein mit'. Perfekt: 'hat mitgebracht' (irregular). Common at parties and dinners — bring something is mandatory etiquette.

words.example: Wir geben zu viel Geld aus.
words.exampleTranslation: We spend too much money.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich gebe Geld aus'. Vowel change: 'du gibst aus, er gibt aus'. 'Einen ausgeben' = to buy someone a drink. Perfekt: 'hat ausgegeben'.

words.example: Ich lade dich zum Essen ein.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm inviting you to dinner.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich lade dich ein'. Vowel change: 'du lädst ein, er lädt ein'. In Germany, 'einladen' often means YOU pay — be ready. Perfekt: 'hat eingeladen'.

words.example: Warte kurz, ich ziehe mich um.
words.exampleTranslation: Wait a moment, I'm changing clothes.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive AND separable. Splits: 'ich ziehe mich um'. Don't confuse with 'umziehen' (move house) — different meanings depending on whether reflexive! Perfekt: 'hat sich umgezogen'.

words.example: Ich schlage einen Kompromiss vor.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm proposing a compromise.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich schlage einen Plan vor'. Vowel change: 'du schlägst vor, er schlägt vor'. Noun 'der Vorschlag' (suggestion). Perfekt: 'hat vorgeschlagen'.

words.example: Das Konzert findet am Samstag statt.
words.exampleTranslation: The concert takes place on Saturday.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'das Konzert findet statt'. Perfekt: 'hat stattgefunden'. Common for events, meetings, performances. No human subject — events 'find their place'.
